content.full_text:
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
John Cook 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322-5662 
City Attorney 
Eureka, CA 
95501 
Dear Mr. Cook: 
Administratian 
322-5660 
Executive/legal 
322-5901 
April 22, 1983 
Enfarcement 
322-6441 
Statements of Ecanomic Interest 
322-6444 
Re: 
Your Telephone Request for 
Advice; 
Our No. A-83-094 
You have requested our advice as to your reporting 
obligations under the following set of circumstances. 
FACTS 
You are a public official (city attorney). 
You also 
maintain a legal practice as a sole practitioner. 
The Act 
requires you to report those clients of your law practice who 
paid you $10,000 or more during the reporting period. 
(Gov. 
Code Sec. 87207(b) (3).) 
This information is disclosed on your 
Statement of Economic Interests (Form 730, Schedule H) . 
QUESTION 
Your question is whether you would have to disclose the name 
of a client whO paid you $9,000 in fees during the reporting 
period and who also paid you $1,500 in reimbursement for costs 
of filing fees and depositions, etc. 
ANSWER 
Governmen t Code Sectio n 87207 (b) (3) l:.I requ ires the 
reporting of the "name of every person from whom the business 
entity received payments if the filer's pro rata share of gross 
!/ All statutory references are to the Government Code 
unless otherwise specified. 


John Cook 
April 22, 1983 
page 2 
receipts from such person was equal to or greater than ... 
$10,000 during a calendar year." 
(Emphasis added.) 
The word 
"payment" in Section 87207(b) (3) includes any payment which 
would constitute income under Section 82030. 
"Income" is 
defined in Section 82030 as "a payment received, including ..• 
reimbursement for expenses. "l/ 
Therefore, a payment of $1,500 as a reimbursement or advance 
for fees and costs from your client must be included as part of 
your gross receipts for disclosure and disqualification 
pu rpose s .1/ 
However, if your client advances funds to cover litigation 
costs and attorneys fees and you deposit these fees into a 
client's trust account, only the funds actually withdrawn by you 
from that account for your fees or reimbursement of costs would 
be included in your gross receipts.i/ Similarly, funds paid 
directly by your client to the court for filing fees or to a 
stenographer for depositions would not be considered part of 
your gross receipts for reporting purposes.~/ 
l/ 
In the case of reporting required by Section 
87207(b) (3), the Commission has specifically held that overhead 
may not be deducted from gross receipts. 
See, Carey Opinion, 
No. 76-087, 3 FPPC Opinions 99, copy enclosed. 
i/ Fees or reimbursements from a client are only counted 
in your gross receipts when the payments have been made to you 
by the client. 
This is also true when costs and fees are 
advanced by your client and kept in your office account until 
dr awn upon. 
i/ If the client has advanced the funds and they are 
deposited into tne clients's trust account, when you are 
authorized to draw upon them the payments are considered as 
being made as of the time when you become entitled to make each 
withdrawal for fees or for reimbursement. 
~/ If State Bar rules permit such costs to be paid 
directly by a check drawn upon the client's trust account, then 
such payments made directly to a court or stenographer would not 
be included in gross receipts. 
We are not in a position to give 
legal advice as to tne proper utilization of a client's trust 
account. 


John Cook 
Apr i 1 22, 1983 
Page 3 
I trust that this letter clarifies the answer to your 
question. 
Should you have further questions, please do not 
hesitate to contact me at (916) 322-5901. 
REL :plh 
Enclosure 
Sincerely, 
~----:; I 
! 
/ 
/--)' 
t- ' 
--~ <.I 
I 
!,/ 
.->-
--~ 
C, (" -~( ! 
'-
-
/' ,,-
'Robert E. Leidign 
Counsel 
Legal Division

sections.conclusion: Government Code Section 87207(b)(3) requires the reporting of the 'name of every person from whom the business entity received payments if the filer's pro rata share of gross receipts from such person was equal to or greater than ... $10,000 during a calendar year.' The word 'payment' in Section 87207(b)(3) includes any payment which would constitute income under Section 82030. 'Income' is defined in Section 82030 as 'a payment received, including ... reimbursement for expenses.' Therefore, a payment of $1,500 as a reimbursement or advance for fees and costs from your client must be included as part of your gross receipts for disclosure and disqualification purposes.
sections.facts:
You are a public official (city attorney). 
You also 
maintain a legal practice as a sole practitioner. 
The Act 
requires you to report those clients of your law practice who 
paid you $10,000 or more during the reporting period. 
(Gov. 
Code Sec. 87207(b) (3).) 
This information is disclosed on your 
Statement of Economic Interests (Form 730, Schedule H) .
sections.question_synthetic: Must a public official attorney disclose a client's name when the client paid $9,000 in fees plus $1,500 in cost reimbursements, totaling $10,500, under Government Code Section 87207(b)(3)?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Yes, the client's name must be disclosed because reimbursements for costs constitute 'income' under Section 82030, and the combined $9,000 in fees plus $1,500 in reimbursements totals $10,500, meeting the $10,000 threshold. However, funds deposited in a client trust account or paid directly by the client to third parties (court, stenographer) are not included in gross receipts.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Clear advice letter with well-defined QUESTION and ANSWER sections. Multiple footnotes provide detailed guidance on trust account treatment and direct payments. Letter dated April 22, 1983, assigned advisory opinion number A-83-094. Signed by Robert E. Leidign, Counsel, Legal Division.

CONCLUSION: Government Code Section 87207(b)(3) requires the reporting of the 'name of every person from whom the business entity received payments if the filer's pro rata share of gross receipts from such person was equal to or greater than ... $10,000 during a calendar year.' The word 'payment' in Section 87207(b)(3) includes any payment which would constitute income under Section 82030. 'Income' is defined in Section 82030 as 'a payment received, including ... reimbursement for expenses.' Therefore, a payment of $1,500 as a reimbursement or advance for fees and costs from your client must be included as part of your gross receipts for disclosure and disqualification purposes.

embedding.summary: FPPC Counsel advises a City Attorney that client cost reimbursements must be included in gross receipts calculations for purposes of determining whether a client's name must be disclosed on Form 730, Schedule H. Since fees of $9,000 plus reimbursements of $1,500 total $10,500, the client's name must be reported under Government Code Section 87207(b)(3).
